pages.raw_text:
DANBURY, Conn, Feb. 26.-The
meager dispatches announcing that
in the celebrated case of Loewe
versus United Hatters, in which
the plaintiff was awarded $4,000
damages, because of boycott, also
intimated that the case would be
appealed to higher courts.


Such move is highly improb-
able, as the hatters have not one
chance in a hundred of securing
a reversal of this latest decree.
The principal involved in this case,
viz. that under the Interpretation
of the Sherman antitrust law it
is illegal for labor unions to boy
colt concerns engaged in inter-
state commerce, was settled two
years ago by the United States;
supreme court.


LAW AGAINST BOYcOTTiNG


The superior court held that
the boycotting is illegal, that
plaintiff is entitled to damages,
and the case was remanded back
to the Connecticut district court
for a hearing as to the amount OF
damages that the defendants must
pay.


1t is therefore practically certain
that the United States supreme
court would not reverse itself re-
garding the principal involved or
even reduce the damages awarded
as excessive.


The Loewe Co. originally sued for
$4,000 damages, which claim has
just been reduced to $4,000, but as
three times the amunt allowed by
a court can be collected under the
Sherman antitrust law, the matters
are actually compelled to pay $222.-
900.


ADD 100000 MORE


Nor is this all. The court costs
extending over a period of six
years, have also been very heavy
approximating at least $4,000.
Add to these sums over $1,000. 900
more that the hatters were COM
PEELED to spendgto save their union
from disruption in bitter war
waged upon them by the allied
manufactures in the east. and 1t Is
found that these workers have
